# Algorithms & Data Structures — F4

> The F4 chapter overview: nine modules from lists through trees, sorting, and hash-based maps to the persistent trie family — HAMT, CHAMP, and the branded CHAMP map keyed by a Snowflake pivot — plus dynamic programming and a lab. F4.01 is built; the rest show their planned dives.

Chapter map for LLMs/agents. Hub: [/elixir/algorithms](/elixir/algorithms). Links are clean root-relative URLs; this file lists every lesson in the chapter in course order.

## Lessons

- [Lists, recursion & complexity — F4.01](/elixir/algorithms/lists): The BEAM list is a linked list of cons cells, not an array: prepend is O(1) and the tail is shared, every list function is written by recursion, and the cost of an…
  - [Cons cells & the shape of a list — F4.01.1](/elixir/algorithms/lists/cons): A cons cell is a head and a tail pointer. [head | tail] builds one new cell over an existing list, so prepend is O(1) and the old list is shared; hd/1 and tl/1 are O(1)…
  - [Recursion over lists — F4.01.2](/elixir/algorithms/lists/recursion): You walk a list by recursion, not a loop: match [h | t], act on the head, recurse on the tail, and stop at []. sum, map, and length are the same shape with a different…
  - [Complexity & big-O on the BEAM — F4.01.3](/elixir/algorithms/lists/big-o): Big-O for a list is concrete: count the cons cells an operation touches. Working at the head is O(1); reaching the end — length, ++, last — is O(n). The cost cheat-sheet…
- [Trees & traversals — F4.02](/elixir/algorithms/trees): A binary tree is a cons cell with two pointers: a node is {value, left, right} or nil. The linear list walk becomes a traversal, and a balanced tree turns an O(n) walk…
  - [Binary trees & recursive shape — F4.02.1](/elixir/algorithms/trees/shape): A node is {value, left, right} or nil, so every tree function handles nil as the base case and a node by combining its two subtrees. size, height, and sum are one…
  - [Depth-first: pre, in, post-order — F4.02.2](/elixir/algorithms/trees/dfs): Depth-first traversal makes the same two recursive calls and differs only in when it visits the node: before the calls (pre), between them (in), or after them (post).…
  - [Breadth-first & balance — F4.02.3](/elixir/algorithms/trees/bfs): Breadth-first traversal walks the tree level by level with a FIFO queue. The level count is the search cost: a balanced tree of n nodes has about log2 n levels, while…
- [Sorting & searching — F4.03](/elixir/algorithms/sorting): Sorting and searching are two halves of one bargain: sort once at O(n log n), and every later lookup drops to an O(log n) binary search. Merge sort and quicksort are the…
  - [Merge & quicksort — F4.03.1](/elixir/algorithms/sorting/sorts): The two workhorse comparison sorts are both divide-and-conquer. Merge sort halves the list, sorts each half, and merges; quicksort picks a pivot, partitions the rest…
  - [Linear & binary search — F4.03.2](/elixir/algorithms/sorting/search): Linear search checks elements one by one over any sequence — O(n). Binary search halves a sorted, randomly-accessible sequence — O(log n) — but a linked list has no O(1)…
  - [Stability & sort cost — F4.03.3](/elixir/algorithms/sorting/cost): Sorts are ranked on average, worst case, space, and stability — whether equal keys keep their order. Over all of them sits a hard floor: a comparison sort is a decision…
- [Maps, sets & hashing — F4.04](/elixir/algorithms/maps): The course you are reading runs on Phoenix LiveView, and its data layer is built from these structures: a map from route to page, a set of built routes, and branded…
  - [Maps & key lookup — F4.04.1](/elixir/algorithms/maps/lookup): A map associates keys with values and looks one up in effectively constant time. Over the course's page registry — a map from route to a page struct — Map.get…
  - [MapSet & membership — F4.04.2](/elixir/algorithms/maps/sets): A MapSet stores unique elements and answers membership in O(1). Over the course's route sets — built versus planned — MapSet.member? is exactly the links gate, and…
  - [Hashing & collisions — F4.04.3](/elixir/algorithms/maps/hashing): Maps and sets reach O(1) by hashing: phash2 turns a key into an integer, which picks a slot, and collisions resolve in place. Elixir stores entries in a 32-way HAMT, so…
- [Hash array mapped tries — F4.05](/elixir/algorithms/hamt): A HAMT spreads a hash table over a tree: it consumes the key's hash five bits at a time, branching up to 32 ways per level, so a node is a bitmap plus one packed array…
  - [Bitmapped nodes — F4.05.1](/elixir/algorithms/hamt/bitmap): A HAMT node keeps one 32-bit bitmap marking which of its slots are occupied and one packed array holding only the occupants, in slot order; a slot's position in the…
  - [Hash-prefix indexing — F4.05.2](/elixir/algorithms/hamt/indexing): A HAMT reads the key's hash in five-bit chunks from the low end: level 0 reads bits 0-4, level 1 reads bits 5-9, each chunk naming one of 32 slots, so a key's path is…
  - [Structural sharing — F4.05.3](/elixir/algorithms/hamt/sharing): An insert builds new nodes only along the path from the root to the changed leaf and shares every other sub-tree by reference, so the old map stays intact and a new…
- [CHAMP maps — F4.06](/elixir/algorithms/champ): A CHAMP is the compressed successor to the HAMT: same O(log32 n) lookup, but each node splits its slots into two bitmaps and two densely packed arrays — entries and…
  - [Compressed node layout — F4.06.1](/elixir/algorithms/champ/layout): A CHAMP node carries a datamap and a nodemap — two bitmaps marking which of its 32 slots hold inline entries and which hold sub-nodes — and stores each kind in its own…
  - [Cache-friendly iteration — F4.06.2](/elixir/algorithms/champ/iteration): Because a CHAMP node keeps its entries contiguous and separate from sub-node pointers, iteration walks the entry array linearly in a canonical order and recurses into…
  - [Canonical equality — F4.06.3](/elixir/algorithms/champ/equality): CHAMP maintains one canonical shape per set of entries, so two equal maps are structurally identical trees and equality short-circuits on shared sub-trees. The same…
- [Identifiers, Snowflake & branded ids — F4.07](/elixir/algorithms/identifiers): Every record in the portal needs a name. A database counter breaks across machines; a random UUID is large and unsortable. A Snowflake is a 64-bit integer carrying its…
  - [Choosing an identifier — F4.07.1](/elixir/algorithms/identifiers/choosing): An auto-increment counter is ordered and tiny but needs one writer, so it cannot scale across machines; a random UUID needs no coordination but is 128-bit and not…
  - [The Snowflake bigint — F4.07.2](/elixir/algorithms/identifiers/snowflake): A Snowflake packs three fields into 64 bits: a 42-bit millisecond timestamp from a custom 2024 epoch, a 10-bit worker id, and a 12-bit per-millisecond sequence. Each…
  - [Branded ids — F4.07.3](/elixir/algorithms/identifiers/branded): A branded id encodes the 64-bit Snowflake in base62 over 0-9A-Za-z, left-pads it to eleven characters, and prepends a three-letter namespace, e.g. PGE0NbWMtkosp8. It is…
- [Branded ids & persistence — F4.08](/elixir/algorithms/persistence): A branded Snowflake is the key everywhere the portal stores data: a 64-bit bigint primary key in SQLite and PostgreSQL, a namespaced string key in Redis. Its decisive…
  - [Branded ids as keys — F4.08.1](/elixir/algorithms/persistence/keys): The database stores the 64-bit integer as a bigint primary key — eight bytes, numerically ordered — and the application brands it into a fourteen-character string only…
  - [SQLite & PostgreSQL — F4.08.2](/elixir/algorithms/persistence/sql): Because the high bits of the id are a timestamp, a window of time is a contiguous window of ids: compute the Snowflake at the window's open and close and query id >= min…
  - [Redis keys — F4.08.3](/elixir/algorithms/persistence/redis): In Redis the id is a namespaced string key, user:USR0NbWMtkosp8. The self-validating id pays off hardest at a cache: an edge validator rejects malformed, out-of-range…
- [Branded CHAMP maps & GenServer — F4.09](/elixir/algorithms/branded-champ): The chapter folds together: the compressed CHAMP of F4.06, the branded Snowflake key of F4.07, and the persistence of F4.08 become one in-memory store — a CHAMP keyed by…
  - [Partition by namespace — F4.09.1](/elixir/algorithms/branded-champ/partition): The Portal's entity registry keeps users, sessions, lessons, and pages in one store: a tiny top-level map from a three-letter namespace to a CHAMP per kind. A lookup…
  - [Structural sharing — F4.09.2](/elixir/algorithms/branded-champ/trie): Inside a partition the CHAMP is keyed on the lesson's Snowflake, and Portal.Progress marks a lesson complete by returning a new map that shares every sub-tree except the…
  - [Own it with a GenServer — F4.09.3](/elixir/algorithms/branded-champ/genserver): The Portal's session store is a CHAMP behind a GenServer. The GenServer serializes writes through its mailbox and publishes each new snapshot; because the map is…
- [Practical recipes in Elixir — F4.10](/elixir/algorithms/recipes): The chapter's structures turned into the Portal's everyday code through three recurring recipes: a with chain to thread a request through validate-load-authorize without…
  - [Idiomatic patterns — F4.10.1](/elixir/algorithms/recipes/patterns): A request to view a lesson clears four gates — validate the id, authenticate the caller, fetch the lesson, authorize access — then renders. Written as a with chain it is…
  - [Streams & pipelines — F4.10.2](/elixir/algorithms/recipes/pipelines): The activity feed wants the three most recent completions for a course. Eager Enum runs each stage to a full list — walking all twelve completions and mapping all seven…
  - [Profiling & complexity — F4.10.3](/elixir/algorithms/recipes/profiling): Every request finds an active session. A list scan is O(n) and grows with the user base; a branded-CHAMP lookup is O(log32 n) and stays a few node hops — 2 at a thousand…
- [Dynamic programming & advanced problems — F4.11](/elixir/algorithms/dynamic-programming): Dynamic programming solves a problem with overlapping subproblems by solving each once and reusing it, in two styles: memoisation caches a recursion top-down, tabulation…
  - [Memoization & overlapping subproblems — F4.11.1](/elixir/algorithms/dynamic-programming/memoization): The longest prerequisite chain to a lesson is one plus the deepest of its prerequisites — a recursion. Because prerequisites are shared, the plain recursion re-derives a…
  - [Tabulation & bottom-up — F4.11.2](/elixir/algorithms/dynamic-programming/tabulation): The fewest modules (worth 1, 3, or 4 credits) summing to a target is one more than the best answer for the target minus a module's worth — optimal substructure.…
  - [Classic DP problems — F4.11.3](/elixir/algorithms/dynamic-programming/problems): Edit distance — the fewest single-character inserts, deletes, or substitutions between two strings — is the textbook two-dimensional DP: a cell per pair of prefixes…
- [Lab: build a branded CHAMP store — F4.12](/elixir/algorithms/lab): The capstone lab assembles the chapter into one Portal.Store: a GenServer over a map from namespace to that namespace's CHAMP. A put is validated, routed to a partition…
  - [Watch a branded CHAMP grow — F4.12.1](/elixir/algorithms/lab/grow): Each put reads a branded id's three-letter namespace and drops the entry into that namespace's partition, creating the partition on first use. Stepping through ten real…
  - [A Snowflake registry — F4.12.2](/elixir/algorithms/lab/registry): Hand the store any branded id and get/1 resolves it in one call: the prefix names the partition, the lookup is an O(log32 n) descent, and the creation time is decoded…
  - [Query by time range — F4.12.3](/elixir/algorithms/lab/range): Because a Snowflake puts the timestamp in its high bits, ids sort by creation time and a time window becomes an id range: the smallest id at time t is (t - epoch) << 22…
